---
title: "Foundations Episode 4 — CI/CD Pipelines (GitHub Actions)"
---

::: {.callout-note}
## Objectives
By the end of this episode you will be able to:

- Explain what CI/CD is and why it catches problems before your teammates do.
- Read and write a GitHub Actions workflow (triggers → jobs → steps).
- Run tests automatically on every push and pull request.
- Build and publish an artifact when the checks pass.

**Teaching:** 45 min · **Exercises:** 30 min
:::

## Why CI/CD?

"It works on my machine" is where bugs go to hide. **Continuous Integration** runs your tests on a
clean machine every time anyone pushes, so a broken change is caught in minutes — not after it's
merged into the robot's control stack. **Continuous Delivery** takes the next step: when the checks
pass, it automatically builds and ships the artifact (a package, a container, this very lesson site).

This repository already does both — `.github/workflows/` renders and deploys the site you're reading.
By the end of this episode you'll understand exactly how.

## Anatomy of a workflow

A workflow is a YAML file in `.github/workflows/`. Three concepts: **when** it runs (`on`), **what**
runs (`jobs`), and the ordered **steps** inside each job:

```yaml
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI
on:                      # WHEN: triggers
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:

jobs:
  test:                  # WHAT: a job runs on a fresh VM
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:               # ordered steps
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4          # get the code
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.11" }
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: pytest -q                     # the gate: fails the build if tests fail
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Instructor note
Open this repo's real `.github/workflows/ci.yml` side by side. Learners trust CI more once they see
it's not magic — it's the same commands they'd run locally, executed on a clean machine. Point out
the `Render (fails the build on errors)` step: that one line is the entire quality gate.
:::

## The build fails when a test fails

The point of the `pytest` step is that a red test turns the whole run red and blocks the merge:

```bash
# a failing test locally → the same failure in CI
pytest -q
# FAILED test_nav.py::test_speed_limit - assert 2.0 <= 1.5
# (exit code 1 → the CI step fails → the PR is blocked)
```

A failing step exits non-zero, GitHub marks the run failed, and branch protection can stop the merge.
That is the safety net: nobody can merge a change that breaks the tests.

## From integration to delivery

Once the checks are green, a second job can **build and publish** an artifact — gated on the first:

```yaml
  deploy:
    needs: test            # only runs if `test` passed
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: ./build.sh                    # produce the artifact
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4   # or deploy: Pages, a registry, a release
        with: { name: site, path: _site }
```

`needs: test` is the link between CI and CD: delivery only happens *after* integration passes, so you
never ship a build that failed its tests.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Challenge 1 — When does it run?
Given the `on:` block above, will the workflow run when you (a) push to `main`, (b) open a pull
request, (c) push to a branch called `experiment` with no PR open?

### Solution
(a) Yes — `push` to `main`. (b) Yes — `pull_request`. (c) No — pushing to `experiment` without a PR
matches neither trigger. Add `experiment` to `push.branches`, or open a PR, to make it run.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Challenge 2 — Why `needs: test`?
What happens to the `deploy` job if a test in the `test` job fails, and why is that the behaviour you
want?

### Solution
`deploy` is skipped — `needs: test` means it only runs if `test` succeeded. You want this because
delivery should never ship an artifact built from code that failed its tests; the dependency makes
"tests pass" a precondition for "publish".
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Key points
- CI runs your tests on a clean machine on every push/PR — it kills "works on my machine".
- A workflow is `on` (when) → `jobs` (what) → `steps` (ordered actions), written as YAML.
- A failing step fails the run and can block the merge — that's the safety net.
- `needs:` chains delivery onto integration: ship the artifact only after the tests pass.
:::

Next: [Foundations Episode 5 — Reproducible Environments](f-05-reproducible-environments.qmd).
